A Hypergraph-Based Framework for Exploratory Business
Intelligence
Yunkai Lou·Shunyang Li·Longbin Lai·Jianke Yu·Wenyuan Yu·

AbstractBusiness Intelligence (BI) analysis is evolv-
ing towardsExploratory BI, an iterative, multi-round ex-
ploration paradigm where analysts progressively refine
their understanding. However, traditional BI systems
impose critical limits forExploratory BI: heavy reliance
on expert knowledge, high computational costs, static
schemas, and lack of reusability. We presentExBI, a
novel system that introduces thehypergraph data
modelwith operators, includingSource,Join, andView,
to enable dynamic schema evolution and materialized
view reuse. Usingsampling-based algorithms with
provable estimation guarantees,ExBIaddresses the
computational bottlenecks, while maintaining analyti-
cal accuracy. Experiments on LDBC datasets demon-
strate thatExBIachieves significant speedups over ex-

KeywordsExploratory Business Intelligence, Hyper-

1 Introduction
Business Intelligence (BI) remains the mainstream data
analysis technology [11,7], with widespread applications
and numerous BI tools [31,38,28]. However, as data
landscapes become increasingly complex and intercon-
nected, BI analysis scenarios are evolving beyond tra-
ditional approaches. Modern analysts often need multi-
round iterative exploration, where each step builds upon
previous insights and progressively refines understand-
ing of the problem.
This exploratory analysis paradigm has gained in-
creasing prominence across various domains. For in-
stance, social scientists have employed such iterative
approaches to investigate the differential impacts of gen-
erative AI on employment [21]. In this paper, we refer to
such analytical scenarios asExploratory BI. To illustrate
the characteristics and challenges ofExploratory BI, we
present a concrete example based on the OpenAIRE
dataset [26], a comprehensive knowledge graph contain-
ing scientific publications, organizations, funding agen-
cies, and their relationships.
Example 1We want to analyze the impact of the Russia-
Ukraine conflict on Russian scientific research.
Step 1:We first examine whether Russian institu-
tions’ publication counts have declined. Analysts mustarXiv:2603.10625v1  [cs.DB]  11 Mar 2026

communicate with database administrators to join the
Publication and Organization tables (Fig. 1a). The anal-
ysis reveals a significant decline starting in 2022.
Step 2:To investigate causes, we hypothesize fund-
ing reductions. This requires initiating a new analysis
workflow with administrators to join the Funding table
(Fig. 1b). Results show that funding from the European
Commission ceased after 2022.
Step 3:We further explore whether funding agen-
cies stopped supporting Russian institutions. This re-
quires yet another cycle with administrators to perform
a self-join on Organization (Fig. 1c). The analysis con-
firms that the European Commission no longer support
Russian institutions, which is confirmed by the Euro-
pean Commission’s official website [9].
1.1 Motivations
The above example demonstrates four critical limita-
tions of existing BI tools, grouped intoLogical Repre-
sentation Gaps(L1-L3) andPhysical System Bot-
tlenecks(L4):
(L1) Heavy Reliance on Analyst Expertise.
Analysts must identify all relevant data upfront and
construct fact/dimension tables, heavily relying on do-
main knowledge [5,18]. This is particularly challeng-
ing when data involvescomplex n-ary relationships
(e.g., the web of authors, papers, and funds), where

(L2) Rigid Schema.Star/snowflake schemas [16,
5] rely on fixed relational structures that are ill-suited
for evolving analysis. Discovering new factors requires
rebuilding the data model, and expressing seemingly
simple semantic constraints (e.g., a funding that sup-
ports Ukraine butnotRussia) demands complex, un-
natural join sequences (e.g., “non-existence” relation-
ship check).
(L3) Lack of Reusability.Current models lack
mechanisms to represent intermediate exploration states
as first-class citizens, preventing reuse across iterations
and comparison with later findings. In Example 1, the
Publication-Organization join was repeated three times.
(L4) Computational Cost.Building wide tables
through table joins is time-consuming and resource-
intensive [29,24], making exploratory analysis imprac-
tical on large datasets. In Example 1, Step 3 joins four
tables, which becomes prohibitively expensive on the
OpenAIRE dataset (193M publications, 51M edges).
Although existing approaches address specific as-
pects of these challenges, they lack a holistic solution
forExploratory BI. Recent LLM-based approaches [20,
15,41] have attempted to lower the expertise barrier
(L1) by directly translating natural language analyti-
cal requirements into query statements. However, their
performances still face significant gaps before practical
deployment. More importantly, these AI-driven solu-
tions merely serve as interfaces and do not address the
fundamental system-level challenges (L2-L4) in the un-
derlying BI systems.
Similarly, mainstream BI platforms like Power BI [31]
and Tableau [38] attempt to mitigate schema rigid-
ity (L2) by enabling ad-hoc view creation via joins.
However, this flexibility comes with severe scalability
and usability constraints. Executing complex multi-way
joins on the fly often incurs prohibitive latency (L4),
rendering interactive exploration impractical. While pre-
materializing these joins as views can improve query re-
sponse, it results in a static collection of artifacts that
cannot adapt to the fluid, unanticipated nature of ex-
ploratory analysis. Furthermore, defining the correct set
of materialized views requires the very domain exper-
tise (L1) thatExploratory BIseeks to democratize.
Therefore, addressing the challenges ofExploratory BI
requires a fundamentally new approach at the system
level, including both the logical and physical aspects,
rather than merely adding intelligent interfaces or ap-
plying workarounds to existing architectures.

A Hypergraph-Based Framework for Exploratory Business Intelligence 3
1.2 Our Approach
In this paper, we proposeExBI, a novel BI system specif-
ically designed to support theExploratory BIscenarios.
ExBIdecouples the logical exploration model from the
physical storage layer to address the aforementioned
challenges comprehensively.
Logically,ExBIaddresses the expressiveness and
flexibility challenges (L1-L3) by introducing aHyper-
graph Data Model. We adopt this graph-centric approach
becauseExploratory BIscenarios involve complex en-
tity relationships that are ill-suited for rigid relational
schemas. Built upon a logical property graph abstrac-
tion, this model allows analysts to definequery graphs
as templates.ExBIemploys aSourceoperator to per-
forminduced subgraph matchingbased on these
templates, capturing even complex semantics like “non-
existence” relationships naturally without cumbersome
join logic. The resulting hypergraphs support dynamic
schema evolution (L2) via theJoinoperator and enable
the materialization of intermediate results as reusable
views (L3). This compositional approach empowers an-
alysts to explore incrementally, reducing the need for
upfront expert schema design (L1).
Physically,ExBItackles the computational bottle-
necks (L4) through a specialized, sampling-based exe-
cution engine. We develop this dedicated engine driven
by two key observations. First, the coreSourceopera-
tor requires subgraph matching, where performingex-
actcomputation is infeasible on large-scale datasets —
whether executed on relational engines (via join trans-
formation [23]) or native graph engines (as validated in
Sec. 6). Second, although sampling-based tools exist for
relational databases (e.g., VerdictDB [30]), they handle
only relatively simple join patterns (limited depth or
without “non-existence” semantics [30]), failing to meet
the complex subgraph matching needs ofExploratory BI.
Consequently,ExBIcurrently implements a custom
storage and computation layer to enable efficient ap-
proximate processing. To implement this layer, we de-
sign specific sampling strategies for our algebraic op-
erators. For the costlySourceoperator, we adapt the
FaSTest sampling algorithm [36] to uniformly sample
matches instead of exhaustive enumeration. For theJoin
operator, we employ stratified group sampling to han-
dle massive intermediate results. Crucially, these tech-
niques provide identifiable statistical guarantees, ensur-
ing thatExBIachieves high performance without com-
promising analytical accuracy. Note that our architec-
ture decouples the execution logic from storage (Sec. 4);
future work will focus on porting our sampling layer to
existing relational and graph databases to reduce mi-
gration overhead for users.We provide a detailed demonstration of howExBI
works through the Russia-Ukraine conflict analysis in
Section 7, illustrating the complete workflow includ-
ing how the hypergraph schema evolves dynamically
through operator composition and how intermediate re-
sults are preserved and reused across analysis rounds.
Note that our design aligns with the recent agent-first
database system proposal [22]. Our incremental explo-
ration framework directly addresses key challenges of
agentic speculation, particularly redundancy, heterogene-
ity and steerability. Building agenticExploratory BIsys-
tems atopExBIis an important future work.
1.3 Contributions
This paper makes the following contributions:
(1)Exploratory BI Paradigm.We analyze the
limitations of traditional BI in supporting exploratory
scenarios and formalize the concept ofExploratory BI.
By shifting the paradigm from one-shot comprehensive
analysis to iterative, incremental exploration, we re-
duce dependency on analyst expertise and enable data-
driven discovery through progressive refinement.
(2)Hypergraph Data Model and Operators.
To support the dynamic nature ofExploratory BI, we in-
troduce the Hypergraph data model along withSource,
Join,View,DrillDown,RollUp,Slice, andDiceoperators
(Sec. 3). This model enables dynamic schema evolu-
tion, reusable intermediate views, and operator compo-
sition for incremental exploration, providing a theoret-
ical foundation for exploratory BI analysis.
(3)System Implementation with Theoretical
Guarantees.We developExBI, a theoretically-grounded
Exploratory BIsystem (Sec. 4) that addresses the perfor-
mance bottlenecks ofSourceandJoinoperators through
sampling-based optimizations. Critically,end-to-end
theoretical guarantees for unbiased estimationof
COUNTandSUMaggregate functions are provided (Sec. 5).
(4)Experimental Evaluation.We conduct ex-
tensive experiments on LDBC datasets to evaluateExBI
(Sec. 6). The results show thatExBIoutperforms exist-
ing systems by a large margin: compared to Neo4j,ExBI
achieves 16.21×average speedup with up to 146.25×
improvement; compared to MySQL, the average speedup
reaches 46.67×with a maximum of 230.53×. Mean-
while,ExBImaintains excellent estimation accuracy, with
only 0.27% average error forCOUNT.

2 Related Work
2.1 Data Cube Models
Traditional BI systems employ the multidimensional
data cube model [12] as their foundational structure.
Data is organized using GROUP BY operations to com-
pute numerical metrics across dimensions. The model
supports OLAP operations includingDrillDown,RollUp,
andSlice, enabling multi-level granularity exploration.
Star and snowflake schemas [16,5] serve as the rela-
tional foundation for implementing data cubes.
As data relationships become increasingly intercon-
nected, graph-based modeling has gained prominence.
Zhao et al. [45] proposed the graph cube model, ex-
tending cube concepts to graph-structured data by ag-
gregating vertices based on attributes while preserving
topology. Related work [10,40] follows similar aggre-
gation approaches. Shi et al. [35] provided a compre-
hensive survey of multidimensional analysis on network
data.
However, these approaches have two fundamental
limitations. First, schemas remain static and require
analysts to define the complete structure upfront, lack-
ing flexibility for incremental adjustment during ex-
ploration. Second, aggregation is limited to vertex at-
tributes and cannot capture structural semantics like
subgraph patterns (e.g., triangles, cliques). In contrast,
our hypergraph model supports dynamic schema evolu-
tion through operator composition and enables pattern-
based retrieval via query graphs.
2.2 Business Intelligence Tools
Business Intelligence has evolved over recent decades
with numerous tools emerging. Leading platforms in-
clude Power BI [31], Tableau [38], QlikSense [33], Ora-
cle BI [28], and Sisense [37]. Despite widespread adop-
tion, these traditional tools share fundamental limita-
tions. First, they require analysts to construct wide ta-
bles upfront by joining all potentially relevant fact and
dimension tables, lacking flexibility to adjust schemas
as understanding deepens. Second, these tools cannot
automatically synthesize results across multiple analy-
sis rounds, leaving the burden of integrating insights
entirely to human analysts.
With Large Language Model (LLM) advancement,
new approaches leverage LLM capabilities for BI. Specif-
ically, NL2SQL work [44,34,39] translates user ques-
tions into SQL queries. NL2BI [20] represents a special
class handling more complex questions. Lian et al. [20]
proposed ChatBI for multi-round dialogues, Jiang etal. [15] proposed SiriusBI with intent querying to un-
derstand user intentions, and Weng et al. [41] proposed
DataLab for various BI tasks.
However, these LLM-based approaches face three
fundamental limitations. First, their performance still
has significant gaps before practical deployment, strug-
gling with complex scenarios. Second, they assume a
single clear mapping between user questions and the
underlying data schema, bypassing the exploratory pro-
cess and case-by-case analysis that are essential when
ambiguity exists. Third, these AI-driven solutions merely
serve as natural language interfaces without addressing
system-level challenges like computational cost, lack of
reusability, and static schema constraints. In contrast,
ourExploratory BIframework tackles these system-level
limitations through a new data model and operator
framework enabling dynamic schema evolution and in-
termediate result reusability.
2.3 Approximate Query Processing
To address computational cost challenges in analytical
workloads, sampling-based approximate query process-
ing (AQP) systems have been proposed. BlinkDB [2]
pre-computes stratified samples of base tables and pro-
vides fast approximate answers with bounded errors
and response times. VerdictDB [30] extends this ap-
proach by providing a middleware layer that universal-
izes AQP across different database backends.
However, these relational AQP systems exhibit fun-
damental limitations when applied toExploratory BIsce-
narios. First, they typically rely onoffline pre-computed
samples(e.g., stratified samples in BlinkDB, scram-
ble tables in VerdictDB). This approach incurs signif-
icant storage and maintenance overhead and lacks the
agility to handle ad-hoc exploratory queries where the
regions of interest are dynamically determined at run-
time. Second, and more critically, they lack the expres-
siveness to handle thestructural complexityof query
patterns inExploratory BI. As evidenced by our exper-
iments, relational sampling approaches still suffer from
severe performance degradation under multi-way joins,
often failing to complete execution within reasonable
time limits. Furthermore, these systems may not sup-
port complex semantics such as “non-existence” in Ver-
dictDB, which is essential for ensuring the induced sub-
graph constraints. In contrast, our hypergraph-based
approach performson-the-fly samplingdirectly over the
data structure without pre-materialization, natively sup-
porting multi-way joins and non-existence semantics
while providing theoretical guarantees for unbiased es-
timation.

A Hypergraph-Based Framework for Exploratory Business Intelligence 5
3 Hypergraph Data Model
Traditional BI systems use star schema and snowflake
schema to model multidimensional data using relational
tables. However, as relationships between data entities
become increasingly interconnected, graph-based data
models emerge as more suitable alternatives. Moreover,
existing data models struggle to support exploratory
scenarios because they are typically static and cannot
accommodate dynamic schema evolution.
In this section, we first introduce our hypergraph
data model, and then present the operators defined on
this model to supportExploratory BI.
3.1 Data Model
We begin by introducing the property graph model,
which serves as the foundation for our hypergraph data
model.
Definition 1 (Property Graph)A property graph is
defined asG= (V G, EG, τG, µG), where:
–VGis the set of vertices,
–E G⊆VG×VGis the set of edges. We write an edge
as(v 1, v2), wherev 1, v2∈V G, and assume undi-
rected edges in this paper for simplicity, meaning
that(v 1, v2)and(v 2, v1)represent the same edge,
–τG:VG∪EG→Lmaps vertices and edges to their
labels, whereLis the set of labels,
–µG: (VG∪EG)×prop→D(prop)maps vertices/edges
to their property values, whereD(prop)denotes the
domain of propertyprop.
In this paper, we usev.propforv∈V Gto denote
the value of propertypropfor vertexv, and similarly
for edges, and hyperedges (Def. 3). When the context
is clear, we writeG= (V, E, τ, µ) (and evenG= (V, E))
for short, and this applies to the following definitions
of hypergraph (Def. 3) and query graph (Def. 4). In
this paper, for ease of exposition, we consider property
graphs as undirected graphs; however, our definitions
can be straightforwardly extended to directed graphs.
Building upon the property graph model, we can
perform multidimensional analysis based on vertex and
edge attributes, similar to traditional data cube mod-
els. However, this approach only supports classification
based on attribute values. In real-world scenarios, we
often need to classify and aggregate not just individual
vertices and edges, but entire subgraphs based on their
structural properties [45]. For instance, computing the
count of all mutually connected groups of three people
requires first identifying all triangular patterns among
Person vertices based on connectivity, then performingaggregation. To address this limitation, we first give the
definition of induced subgraphs and hyperedges, then
propose the hypergraph data model.
Definition 2 (Induced Subgraph)Given a property
graphG= (V, E),G sub= (V s, Es)is an induced sub-
graph ofGif and only if: (1)V s⊆VandE s⊆E; (2)
∀(u, v)∈E, ifu∈V sandv∈V s, then(u, v)∈E s.
Definition 3 (Hypergraph Data Model)Given a
property graphG= (V G, EG, τG, µG), we denoteG ⟨G⟩=
(VG,EG, µG)as a hypergraph regardingG, where:
–VG=VG,
–EGis the set of hyperedges, where each hyperedge
encode a set of graph verticesV ϵ, denoted asV ϵ⊆
VG,
–µG:EG×prop→D(prop)maps hyperedges to their
property values.
When the underlying property graphGis not of
concern, we abbreviate the hypergraph asG. It is im-
portant to note that a hyperedge is not merely a set
of vertices—given the underlying graphG, the vertices
in a hyperedge form an induced subgraph ofG, which
can exhibit structural characteristics. The properties
of hyperedges, defined throughµ G, can capture such
structural information, as we will demonstrate when
introducing the hypergraph schema (Def. 7).
Example 2Consider a property graph containing two
types of vertices: Publication (abbr. Pub) and Organi-
zation (abbr. Org), as shown in Fig. 2b. Pub vertices
can be connected by Cite edges, while Pub and Org ver-
tices can be connected by hasAuthorInstitution edges
(abbr. HAI). Fig. 2a shows a property graph instance
with four Pub vertices and three Org vertices. Fig. 2d
shows the corresponding hypergraph with two hyperedges
ϵ1andϵ 2. Take hyperedgeϵ 1as an example: it contains

3.2 Operators on Hypergraph Data Model
We first present three core operators based on the hy-
pergraph data model, namely,Source,Join, andView,
and then discuss the adaptations of traditional cube op-
erators to hypergraphs. These operators exhibit a clo-
sure property:Sourceserves as the entry point (Graph
→Hypergraph),Viewas the exit point (Hypergraph→
Table), while all others (Join,DrillDown,RollUp,Slice,
Dice) form a closed algebra (Hypergraph→Hyper-
graph), enabling composable chains for iterative explo-
ration.

3.2.1SourceOperator
Before defining theSourceoperator, we introduce the
concept of induced subgraph isomorphism.
Definition 4 (Query Graph)A query graph is de-
fined asq= (V q, Eq, τq), whereV qis the set of vertices,
Eqis the set of edges, andτ qmaps vertices and edges
to their labels.
The query graph serves as a template for subgraph
matching. Optionally, users can specify constraints on
vertex and edge property values to filter matching re-
sults. Fig. 2c shows an example of a query graph with
three verticespub 0, pub 1, org 0. The two Pub vertices are
required to have publication dates after January, 2020.
To perform pattern matching with query graphs,
we adopt the induced subgraph isomorphism semantics
following Carletti et al. [3]. This ensures that match-
ing results precisely capture the connectivity structure
specified in the query graph.
Definition 5 (Induced Subgraph Isomorphism)
Given a property graphG= (V, E, τ, µ)and a query
graphq= (V q, Eq, τq), a subgraphG s= (V s, Es)⊆G
is an induced subgraph isomorphic toqif there exists a
bijectionf:V q→V ssuch that:
–Label preservation:∀u∈V q,τq(u) =τ(f(u)),
–Edge preservation:∀(u 1, u2)∈E q,(f(u 1), f(u 2))
∈Esandτ q((u1, u2)) =τ((f(u 1), f(u 2))),
–Induced structure:G sis an induced subgraph of
G, and∀(v 1, v2)∈E s,(f−1(v1), f−1(v2))∈E qand
τ((v 1, v2)) =τ q((f−1(v1), f−1(v2))).
The bijectionfis called a matching, or matched in-
stance ofqinG. If property constraints are specified in
q, the matching must also satisfy these constraints.Note that we do not require the query graph to be
connected. Ifqis disconnected, the induced subgraph
constraint ensures that matched subgraphs must also
be disconnected. This design choice allows analysts to
explicitly encode disconnectivity as an important struc-
tural constraint in exploratory analysis.

For each hyperedgeϵ, the property mapping func-

Gs. Specifically,µ G(ϵ,topology)(orϵ.topology) is deter-

Sq=Vq∪Eq∪ PG
whereV qrepresents query vertices (vertex columns),E q

For each hyperedgeϵin the hypergraph, there exists

following three types of columns based on this matching:
–Vertex columns: For each query vertexu∈V q,

vertex using the notationu.prop, which denotes the

–Edge columns: For each query edge(u 1, u2)∈E q,
the corresponding edge(f(u 1), f(u 2))exists in the
induced subgraph. We can access its properties using
(u1, u2).prop, which denotes the value ofpropfor

nameprop∈ P G, we can access the property value

The hypergraph schema represents the structural
and property information maintained throughout hy-
pergraph data model operations. The association be-
tween a hypergraph and its query graph can be estab-
lished in two ways: (1) explicitly through theSourceop-
erator (Def. 6), where pattern matching against query
graphqgenerates hyperedges with their matchings; (2)
implicitly through theJoinoperator (Def. 9), which
composes two query graphs to form an extended query
graph while maintaining schema consistency. Through-
out all operations, the schema preserves its three-part
structure of vertex, edge, and hyperedge columns.
The hypergraph schema provides a uniform view
over all hyperedges: each hyperedge can be conceptu-
ally viewed as a row in a relational table, where columns
correspond to query vertices, query edges, and hyper-
edge properties. Cell values for vertex and edge columns
reference the actual vertices and edges in the underlying
property graphGthrough the matchingf, while hyper-
edge columns contain structural and other properties of
the subgraphs themselves. This schema-based view nat-
urally enables access to both graph properties fromG
and structural properties of hyperedges, and serves as
the foundation for bridging hypergraphs and traditional
data cube models (see Sec. 3.2.3 and Sec. 3.2.4).Remark 1 (Structural Properties of Hyperedges)The hy-
peredge properties inP Gcan capture structural charac-
teristics of the induced subgraphs. A key example is the
built-in topology property (introduced in Def. 6), which
describes the connectivity pattern among vertices in a
hyperedge. For instance, consider a hyperedge contain-
ing three vertices induced from a query graph with three
edges. The induced subgraph can form different topolo-
gies depending on which edges are actually present in
G:
– Triangle: all three vertices are mutually connected
(all three edges present)
– 2-path: vertices form a linear chain (exactly two
edges present)
– Disconnected: fewer than two edges present, or
vertices are completely isolated
Such topology can be efficiently encoded as a bitset
where each bit represents edge presence (1 for con-
nected, 0 for disconnected) between vertex pairs. This
encoding enables efficient classification and aggrega-
tion based on graph patterns, which is essential for
Exploratory BIanalysis. More generally, we can consider
incorporating other graph analytics (e.g., community
detection) to define theSource, as well as additional
structural properties such as modularity score and den-
sity. We will explore these possibilities in future work.

In theJoinoperator, we explicitly define the two hyper-

andG′. This design avoids complications when vertices

3.2.3ViewOperator
While the hypergraph structure provides flexibility for
modeling complex and dynamic schema inExploratory

A Hypergraph-Based Framework for Exploratory Business Intelligence 9
BI, it can be challenging to directly analyze and inter-
pret. To leverage the existing BI ecosystem—including
traditional BI techniques, aggregate functions, and vi-
sualization toolkits—we introduce theViewoperator
that projects hypergraphs into relational tables, en-
abling seamless integration with established BI tools.
Definition 10 (ViewOperator,View)Given a hyper-
graphG ⟨G⟩= (V G,EG, µG)with query graphq= (V q, Eq,
τq)and schemaS q=Vq∪Eq∪ PG, the operatorView:
G ⟨G⟩ →RprojectsG ⟨G⟩into a relational tableR
where each hyperedgeϵwith matchingfcorresponds to
one row. The columns are derived from vertex, edge,
and hyperedge properties as defined inS q.
In general, when hyperedges in a hypergraph are
generated from different query graphs, projecting them
into a single relational table requires constructing a
unified schema that accommodates all vertex and edge
properties, with NULL values for missing columns. In
this paper, we simplify by assuming all hyperedges in
a hypergraph are generated from the same query graph
(ensured by ourSourceandJoinoperators). However,
hyperedges may still contain different numbers of edges
depending on the topology of their induced subgraphs.
For example, given a query graph with three vertices
and three edges, one hyperedge might form a triangle
(all three edges present), while another forms a path
(only two edges present). We can handle such varying
edge structures by including all possible edges from the
query graph in the schema and setting absent edges to
NULL.

3.2.4 Adaptation to Cube Operators
To support multi-dimensional analysis on hypergraphs,
we can conceptually turn the hypergraph schema into
a relational view as in theViewoperator, apply tradi-
tional BI cube operators [12] such asDrillDown,RollUp,
Slice, andDice, and then transform back to the hyper-
graph representation—all without changing the physi-
cal data layout. These operators do not alter the under-
lying query graph but rather operate at the schema level
by manipulating which properties are used for grouping
and aggregation.
For example,DrillDownrefines the analysis by in-
creasing granularity—moving from coarser to finer lev-
els of detail (e.g., drilling down to specific vertex prop-
erties and even topologies). Conversely,RollUpreduces
granularity by aggregating hyperedges at higher levels.Slicefilters hyperedges by fixing specific property values
on one dimension, whileDiceselects hyperedges satis-
fying constraints across multiple dimensions. Since all
hyperedges in a hypergraph share the same query graph
structure (ensured by ourSourceandJoinoperators),
they naturally project to a uniform relational schema,
enabling seamless application of these cube operators.
Therefore, we omit their formal definitions in this pa-
per.
4 Framework Overview
To supportExploratory BIand address the challenges
discussed in Section 1, we design and implement the
ExBIsystem. As illustrated in Fig. 5, theExBIframe-
work consists of three main layers: Storage Layer, Com-
putational Layer, and Analysis Layer. In the following
subsections, we detail the functionality and design of
each layer.
4.1 Storage Layer
The Storage Layer is responsible for data persistence
and management. Although our Hypergraph Data Model
is logically built on a property graph abstraction (see
Sec. 3), the underlying storage architecture is designed
to be agnostic. In principle, any database system capa-
ble of supporting induced subgraph matching — whether
a native graph database (e.g., Neo4j [25]) or a relational
database (via schema mapping [23]) — can serve as the
backend.
However, as highlighted in Sec. 1, generic database
engines currently fall short of the performance require-
ments forExploratory BIon large-scale datasets. Rela-
tional engines incur high costs for complex join-based
matching, while native graph databases lack the spe-
cialized sampling primitives needed for efficient estima-
tion. Consequently,ExBIcurrently implements a cus-
tom storage layer. We build this layer by extending the
DAF algorithm [13] to rigorously support induced sub-
graph matching [3] and integrating it directly with our
sampling-based optimization engine. This implementa-
tion serves as a performance enabler, allowingExBIto
meet interactive analysis demands while retaining the
architectural flexibility to support standard databases
in the future.
4.2 Computational Layer
The Computational Layer serves as the core ofExBI,
managing the interactions with both the Storage Layer

Fig. 5, this layer comprises two key components: the

Hypergraph Operation Engine. The Hypergraph Op-
eration Engine implements all operators defined on the
hypergraph data model, e.g.,Source,Join, andViewetc.
These operators enable dynamic schema evolution and
iterative analysis by transforming hypergraphs to in-
corporate new dimensions or refine analytical focus.
Among these operators, theSourceoperator plays a
unique role. While most operators take hypergraphs as
both input and output, theSourceoperator takes the
graph database as input and produces a hypergraph
as output. It constructs hypergraphs from the underly-
ing property graph by performing subgraph matching
based on query patterns (as defined in Def. 6). There-
fore, theSourceoperator serves as the bridge connect-
ing the Storage Layer and the Computational Layer,
enabling data retrieval and hypergraph construction.
Additionally, theViewoperator bridges the Computa-
tional Layer and the BI Analysis Layer by taking a
hypergraph as input and producing a relational table
as output, which is then passed to the Analysis Layer
for analysis.
Hypergraph Tree. The Hypergraph Tree maintains a
record of hypergraphs generated during the exploratory
analysis process. Each node in the tree represents a hy-pergraph, and child nodes are derived from their par-
ent nodes through the application of operators from the
Hypergraph Operation Engine. For example, in Fig. 5,
the hypergraph ofTN 1is obtained by applying theJoin
operator to the hypergraph of its parent nodeTN 0, in-
corporating a new data dimension for analysis.
This tree structure captures the evolution of analyt-
ical understanding throughout the exploration process,
enabling analysts to review previous states, compare
analytical paths, and reuse intermediate results. Ana-
lysts can select hypergraphs from the tree, apply oper-
ators to generate new hypergraphs, and pass them to
the Analysis Layer for examination.
4.3 Analysis Layer
The Analysis Layer receives the relational table from
the Computational Layer, which is obtained with the
Viewoperator, and then performs aggregation and anal-
ysis on it. This transformation enables the application
of traditional BI aggregation operations and facilitates
result interpretation. Specifically, analysts select spe-
cific dimensions (columns) for grouping and apply ag-
gregate functions to compute measures of interest. The
aggregate functions currently supported in our system
includeCOUNT,DISTINCT COUNT,MAX,MIN, andSUM.
These aggregation functions cover typical BI analysis
scenarios [42,43]. In the future, we will consider sup-
porting additional aggregate functions.
We have designed an analysis interface to integrate
the analytical capabilities of existing BI tools such as
Power BI [31] and Tableau [38]. Specifically, if the con-
vert function in the interface is implemented in an ex-
isting BI tool, the relational table transformed by the
Viewoperator can be converted into the internal data
structure of that BI tool, thereby enabling the use of
existing BI tools to perform subsequent analytical tasks
on the relational table.
Importantly, insights derived from the Analysis Layer
can guide subsequent operations in the Computational
Layer. Based on analytical results, analysts may iden-
tify the need to incorporate additional data dimensions
(throughJoinoperations), refine the data scope (through
Sliceoperations), or adjust the granularity of analysis
(throughDrillDownorRollUpoperations). This feed-
back loop between the Analysis Layer and the Com-

A Hypergraph-Based Framework for Exploratory Business Intelligence 11
putational Layer embodies the essence of exploratory
analysis, enabling analysts to iteratively refine their un-
derstanding and progressively discover insights.
5 Operator Implementation
This section describes how to implement the operators
in the computational layer ofExBIintroduced in the
previous section. Specifically, since operators inherited
from traditional BI, such asDrillDownandSlice, can
be naturally implemented following traditional BI ap-
proaches, we focus on the implementation of three new
operators:Source,Join, andView.
5.1 Basic Implementation of New Operators
SourceOperator. According to Def. 6, theSourceop-
erator performs subgraph matching on the property
graph to find matching results, which form hyperedges,
thereby obtaining a hypergraph. The implementation
directly invokes the subgraph matching algorithm in
the storage layer, as described in Section 4.
JoinOperator. TheJoinoperator merges two hyper-
graphs into one, merging hyperedges based on the join
condition in the process. An intuitive method is as fol-
lows: for two hypergraphsG 1= (VG1,EG1, κG1, µG1) and
G2= (V G2,EG2, κG2, µG2) participating in the join, for
each hyperedge inG 1, iterate through the hyperedges in
G2to determine whether these two hyperedges satisfy
the join condition. When the join condition is satisfied,
add the new hyperedge to the resulting hypergraph.
This join implementation method is calledJOIN cmpl.
Note that join performance can be improved by build-
ing indexes on the vertex properties, edge properties,
and hyperedge properties.
ViewOperator. TheViewoperator transforms a hy-
pergraph into a relational table, enabling the applica-
tion of traditional BI methods for hypergraph analysis.
Specifically, given a hypergraph, theViewoperator it-
erates through its hyperedges, and for each hyperedge,
projects it into a row in the relational table according
to Def. 10, thereby obtaining the final relational table.
5.2 Performance Challenges
The above implementations ofSourceandJoinface sig-
nificant performance challenges in supporting interac-
tive exploratory analysis. InExploratory BI, analysts typ-
ically need to repeatedly invoke these operators—for ex-
ample, continuously callingSourceto obtain new hyper-
graphs, or callingJoinmultiple times to merge hyper-
graphs for iterative analysis. Therefore, the executionefficiency of these operators is critical to ensure smooth
analytical workflows. Unfortunately, the straightforward
implementations can suffer from critical performance is-
sues.
SourceOperator Challenges. For theSourceoperator,
since subgraph matching is an NP-complete problem [32],
implementing an exact algorithm to find all match-
ing results is very time-consuming. In our experiments,
finding 4-path with four vertices in a graph with only
9,892 vertices takes about 4 minutes. Additionally, sub-
graph matching may produce a large number of results.
If hyperedges corresponding to each result are all con-
structed and stored in the hypergraph, this will also
lead to unacceptable memory overhead.
JoinOperator Challenges. TheJoinoperator faces sim-
ilar problems. As mentioned above, when hyperedges
inG 1andG 2are sorted according to the join condi-
tion, the time complexity of theJOIN cmplalgorithm is
O(|EG1|+|EG2|). However, asExploratory BIanalysis pro-
gresses, the number of hyperedges in hypergraphs may
increase dramatically, leading to poor performance of
JOIN cmpl. Specifically, on the one hand, theSourceop-
erator performing subgraph matching may result in a
large number of hyperedges in the hypergraph; on the
other hand, multiple joins may also cause the number
of edges in the hypergraph to grow (especially when the
join conditionσ=∅, i.e., performing a Cartesian prod-
uct). Moreover, during theExploratory BIanalysis pro-
cess, sorting or building indexes for hyperedges in the
hypergraph is itself time-consuming. When hyperedges
in the hypergraph are unsorted, the time complexity of
theJOIN cmplalgorithm rises toO(|E G1| · |EG2|), making
it difficult to meet practical computation requirements.
5.3 Sampling-Based Solutions
To address the performance challenges faced by the
SourceandJoinoperators, we adopt sampling-based
approaches that compute results on a curated subset
rather than exhaustively materializing all matches. The
key motivation for sampling-based solutions stems from
the nature of BI analysis: analysts are typically inter-
ested in aggregated values (e.g., COUNT, SUM) rather
than individual data records [1]. Once these aggregates
can be accurately approximated from samples usingun-
biasedestimators, computing the complete result set
becomes unnecessary. We first introduce the sampling
methods for botherSourceandJoinoperators.
Sampling forSourceOperator. For theSourceopera-
tor, we adapt the FaSTest algorithm [36] to perform
sampling during the subgraph matching process, result-
ing in a new implementation calledSOURCE fas. This

a key challenge in applying them to theExBIsystem is
ensuring end-to-end accurate estimation after integrat-
ing these sampling algorithms into the system.
5.4 Unbiased Estimation Guarantee
In this subsection, we will prove that after applying
these sampling algorithms, when computing aggregate
functions likeCOUNTandSUM, we can obtain unbiased
estimates of the true values, thereby demonstrating the
feasibility of our approach. Meanwhile, for other ag-
gregate functions such asDISTINCT COUNT,QUANTILE,
MAX, andMIN, to the best of our knowledge, there are
no established unbiased estimation methods. Therefore,
we adopt existing approximate estimation techniques
and try to ensure an error bound for the estimation:
forDISTINCT COUNT, we use the GEE estimator [4]; for
QUANTILE, we employ the t-digest method [8]. More-
over, forMAXandMIN, we use the maximum and mini-
mum values from the sampled data as estimates. Pleasenote that these approximate estimation methods such
as GEE estimator and t-digest method require uni-
formly sampled data as input for a better estimation.

6 Experimental Evaluation
In this section, we first describe the experimental set-
tings (Sec. 6.1), including datasets, queries, and com-
pared methods. We then evaluate the performance of
ExBIthrough extensive experiments Sec. 6.2–Sec. 6.6.6.1 Experimental settings
Dataset. We use the LDBC Social Network Benchmark-
ing (SNB) [19] dataset for our experiments, as LDBC is
widely adopted for graph algorithms benchmarking [17,
23] and provides large-scale property graphs suitable for
our evaluation. The dataset consists of varying vertices
(e.g.,Person,Post,Comment,Place) and edges (e.g.,
knows,hasCreator,isLocatedIn). Among them, the
knowstable (representing friendship relations among
persons) and thehasCreatortable (representing au-
thorship of posts/comments) are two of the largest re-
lations. We evaluate our approach on four scale factors:
SF0.1, SF1, SF3, and SF10. The details of the datasets
are shown in Table 1, and the largest dataset SF10 con-
tains approximately 30 million vertices and 177 million
edges. We do not extend our experiments to larger-
scale datasets because non-sampling approaches (e.g.,
SOURCE cmplandJOIN cmpl) already fail in many cases to
complete queries at SF10. Consequently, obtaining the
exact ground truth required to evaluate the sampling
error can be infeasible at larger datasets.
Exploratory BI queries. Since there are no publicly avail-
able exploratory BI query benchmarks, we design 13
queries (Q1–Q13) based on IC queries [19] on the LDBC
datasets to evaluate our approach. The queries are shown
in Fig. 6. Each query consists of two query graphs con-
nected through join conditions, which are depicted as
yellow dashed lines. For instance, in Q1 (Fig. 6(a)), the
Personvertex in the left query graph (Q1 Left) is joined
with thePersonvertex in the right query graph (Q1
Right) onPerson.ID, as indicated by the yellow dashed
line. Note that for each query, we performSourceon
both the left and right query graphs separately. Since
the two source operations are similar in nature, we re-
port the results for the left query graph by default in our
experiments. Furthermore, we evaluate commonly used
aggregate functions includingDISTINCT COUNT,COUNT,
andMAXfor each query. We limit most experiments to
joins between two query graphs due to the computa-
tional cost of exact approaches. In Sec. 6.6, we further
evaluate the performance of multiple joins.
Compared methods. We compare the following meth-
ods in our experiments: (1)ExBI: Our full system with
sampling-based operators (SOURCE fasandJOIN sgs) for
efficient approximate query processing. (2)ExBI cmpl: A
variant ofExBIthat replacesSOURCE fasandJOIN sgs
with their exact counterpartsSOURCE cmplandJOIN cmpl,
serving as an ablation baseline. (3)MySQL [27]: A
widely-used traditional relational database system. (4)
Neo4j [25]: A popular graph database system. More-
over, we use a sampling-based method as a baseline, i.e.,
VerdictDB [30], which is an approximate relational

6.3 Comparison with Exact Methods
In this section, we compare the end-to-end performance
ofExBIwith exact methods (ExBI cmpl, MySQL, and
Neo4j) on the SF1, SF3, and SF10 datasets. As shown in
Fig. 8,ExBIcompletes all 13 queries across all datasets
within reasonable time, with execution times ranging
from 10.12s (Q 12on SF1) to 335.94s (Q 3on SF10).
In contrast, exact methods increasingly fail as dataset
size grows.ExBI cmplencountersOOMerrors: 5/13 queries
fail on SF1, 7/13 on SF3, and 9/13 on SF10. This
is primarily due to the combinatorial explosion of in-
termediate results inherent in exact subgraph match-
ing. For instance,Q 6on SF1 produces over 27 billion
results, making the full materialization of exact an-
swers prohibitively memory-intensive. Neo4j, as a na-
tive graph database, performs relatively well but still
encountersOOTerrors on complex queries: 3/13 fail on
SF1, 6/13 on SF3, and 6/13 on SF10. MySQL shows
worse scalability due to the complexity of multi-way
joins: 6/13 queries fail on SF1, 10/13 on SF3, and 11/13
on SF10. These results show that many queries are dif-
ficult to complete within reasonable time using exact
algorithms, validating the necessity of sampling-based
approaches. On queries where both MySQL and Neo4j
complete, Neo4j consistently outperforms MySQL by
3–260×(e.g.,Q 4: 29.81s vs. 2911.05s;Q 10: 46.75s vs.
6009.28s;Q 11: 18.90s vs. 4935.93s on SF1). This per-
formance gap underscores the inherent efficiency of the
graph data model for structural queries, validating our
choice to buildExBIupon a graph-based foundation.
In comparison with Neo4j,ExBIdelivers an aver-
age speedup of 16.21×and a maximum speedup of
146.25×(forQ 9on SF1). Compared to MySQL,ExBI
achieves higher speedups, i.e., an average speedup of
46.67×and a maximum speedup of 230.53×(forQ 10
on SF1). While Neo4j exhibits lower latency on simple
queries (e.g.,Q 12on SF1), this is an artifact of our fixed
experimental configuration rather than a system limi-
tation. Specifically, for queries with small ground-truth
cardinalities (e.g., 361k forQ 12on SF1), our default
1M sample size results in over-sampling, incurring pro-
cessing overhead with no accuracy gain. By aligning the
sample size with the query selectivity (e.g., 10K sam-
ples),ExBIreduces latency to 1.02s (with only 1.30% er-
ror). We will study query-adaptive sampling techniques
in future work.
Table 6 shows the estimation error rates ofExBI
across different datasets and aggregate functions. NotethatQ 1,Q3, andQ 5are not reported in this table
because exact algorithms fail to complete on all three
datasets, making it impossible to obtain ground truth
values for error calculation. ForCOUNTandMAX,ExBI
achieves consistently low error rates across all queries
and datasets (mostly below 1%). ForDISTINCT COUNT,
ExBIshows higher error rates due to the use of a biased
estimator (Sec. 6.2). The results demonstrate thatExBI
maintains high accuracy while successfully completing
all 13 queries that exact methods cannot handle.
Overall, these results show thatExBIsignificantly
reduces execution time while maintaining comparable
accuracy compared to the baselines, making it highly
suitable for practical complex query workloads.
6.4 Comparison with VerdictDB
In this section, we compareExBIwith VerdictDB, a
representative sampling-based AQP system.
VerdictDB configurations. WhileExBIapplies sampling
dynamically during query execution, VerdictDB pre-
computes materialized sample tables with fixed sam-
pling rates. We evaluate three VerdictDB configura-
tions:
– Ver 1%: Follows the recommended settings in [30],
pre-constructing sample tables with 1% uniform sam-
ples and 1% universe samples for large tables;
– Ver 10%: Pre-constructs sample tables with 10% sam-
pling rate for more accurate results;
– Ver all: Uses 100% data. VerdictDB employs early
stopping during computation, which terminates when
the estimated error falls below a threshold, so Ver all
is still not an exact algorithm.
On small datasets (SF1 and SF3), all tables do not qual-
ify as “large” so VerdictDB skips pre-sampling and op-
erates on the original tables. Consequently, all configu-
rations reduce to Ver alland produce identical results.
On SF10, VerdictDB’s pre-sampling strategy activates
on the large tables (e.g.knowstable), causing different
results across configurations.
UnlikeExBI, which computes all aggregates (COUNT,
MAX, andDISTINCT COUNT) in one query, VerdictDB
necessitates different sample types for different aggre-
gates: universe samples forDISTINCT COUNTand uni-
form samples forCOUNTandMAX. Consequently, Ver-
dictDB cannot compute all aggregates in a single pass.
In our experiments, we execute two separate queries for
VerdictDB—one forDISTINCT COUNTand another for
COUNTandMAX—and report the maximum of the two
execution times as VerdictDB’s query time. If either
query times out, the result is recorded asOOT. Note that
we only report VerdictDB results forQ 11–Q13because

Q12,ExBIis slower than VerdictDB because of the sam-

in SF3. Regarding accuracy, VerdictDB achieves near-

Results on SF10 (Table 8). ExBIefficiently completes
all three queries with stable error rates:COUNTerrorsremain below 0.21%,MAXerrors are consistently 0.00%,
andDISTINCT COUNTerrors lie between 37.59% and
43.54% (due to the biased estimator). In contrast, Ver-
dictDB exhibits critical limitations. First, When pre-
sampling is not used, VerdictDB faces severe scalability
bottlenecks: Ver alltimes out on the complex queryQ 13
that involves a 4-clique. Second, while pre-sampling can
alleviate these scalability issues, it introduces structural
fragility. Ver 1%returns “None” results (marked asNA)
forCOUNTandMAXonQ 11andQ 13, because the 1%
uniform sampling rate breaks the connectivity of cyclic
subgraphs. This hypothesis is confirmed by Ver 10%: in-
creasing the sampling rate restores enough connectivity
to produce results forQ 11. Third, even in cases where
VerdictDB produces outputs, it still suffers from ex-
treme statistical instability. For example, Ver 10%re-
turns a result forQ 11, but with a 99.92%COUNTer-
ror. Similarly, the universe sampling strategy used for
DISTINCT COUNTyields significant errors: 3.44×105%
forQ 11and 1.30×1010% forQ 13under Ver 1%, render-
ing the results useless in practice.
Comparatively,ExBIoffers a much more robust so-
lution for complex queries required forExploratory BI,
balancing performance and accuracy where baselines
fail.

To provide a more detailed performance analysis, we

sampling-based and non-sampling operators.
Source Operator. As shown in Fig. 9a,SOURCE fassuc-

7 Case Study
We demonstrateExBIthrough a comprehensive analy-
sis of the question “What is the impact of the Russia-
Ukraine conflict on Russian scientific research?” on the
OpenAIRE dataset. The details are shown in Fig. 11.
Phase 1: Case Establishment. To investigate whether
the Russia-Ukraine conflict affected Russian scientific
research, we first need to establish the case. We use
Sourceto construct a Publication-Organization hyper-
graph, then applyDrillDownon bothorg.countryand
pub.yeardimensions. This yields the number of publi-
cations per country per year, revealing a notable decline
in Russian institutions’ output since 2022. However,
this alone does not confirm causation. To verify this
is Russia-specific rather than a global trend, we per-
formDrillDownonly onpub.year(without the country
dimension), obtaining global publication counts. Thecomparison shows that worldwide publications increased
in 2022, while Russian output dropped significantly—
strongly suggesting the decline is linked to the conflict.
Phase 2: Divergent and In-depth Exploration. Having
established the case, follow-up questions naturally arise,
such asQ1: What are the reasons for the decline?and
Q6: Is the decline related to specific research fields?
Taking Q1 as an example, we explore possible causes
includingresource factors,publishing environment, and
author-related factors, and conduct in-depth exploration
for each.
Resource Factors.Relevant resources may include
funding, international cooperation, etc. Taking fund-
ing as an example, we first explore what are the main
funding sources for Russian research (Q2-1). We ex-
tend the hypergraph viaJoinwith Funding data—note
that this reuses the Publication-Organization hyper-
graph obtained in Q0. Query Q2-1 reveals that the Eu-
ropean Commission (EC) was a major funder of Rus-
sian institutions.
Next, we investigate whether EC’s funding for Rus-
sian research changed around 2022 (Q2-2). We extend
Q2-1’s result with another Organization entity to ex-
amine whether EC ceased funding Russia while con-
tinuing to support Ukraine. The results show that in
2022, EC ceased funding projects involving both Rus-

A Hypergraph-Based Framework for Exploratory Business Intelligence 23
sian and Ukrainian institutions simultaneously (the cor-
responding row shows zero), while continuing to fund
Ukraine-only projects (26 organizations). This suggests
EC’s policy shift, which is confirmed by official EC doc-
umentation [9].
Publishing Environment.Here, publishing environ-
ment primarily refers to the attitudes of journals and
conferences towards Russian research. To explore this
factor, we propose Q4-1, reusing the hypergraph ob-
tained in Q0 and applyingDrillDownon thepub.year
andpub.publisherdimensions. Then, aSliceis ap-
plied with the conditionorg.country = "RU"to ex-
amine how many papers each publisher published from
Russian institutions per year. The results reveal that
EDP Sciences dramatically reduced publications from
Russian institutions after 2022. Investigating further,
we found that EDP Sciences publicly announced sup-
port for Ukraine1, reflecting an increasingly challenging
publishing environment for Russian researchers. Fur-
thermore, to verify whether EDP Sciences specifically
reduced only Russian publications in 2022, we conduct
Q4-2 for additional validation.
Author Factors.Similarly, we consider author-related
factors, which may include author count, productiv-
ity, etc. In this example, we focus on author count
and compare changes in the number of Russian authors
who published papers (Q5-1) versus authors from other
countries (Q5-2) to determine whether author factors
contribute significantly to the decline in Russian pub-
lications. Specifically, for Q5-1, we introduce the Au-
thor dimension into the hypergraph obtained from Q0.
The query results reveal that the number of Russian
researchers who published papers in 2022 decreased by
15% compared to 2021, suggesting that author factors
may be a contributing cause to the decline in Russian
publications.
Benefits ofExBI. This case study demonstratesExBI’s
core advantages: (1)Reduced expertise dependency:
analysts explored hypotheses iteratively rather than hav-
ing to define all factors upfront. Furthermore, build-
ing agentic systems atopExBIto assist in query for-
mulation is a key direction for future work; (2)Dy-
namic schema evolution: we progressively incorpo-
rated funding, publisher, and author dimensions with-
out rebuilding the base model; (3)Result reusability:
the Publication-Organization hypergraph was reused
across all analyses. These advantages effectively address
three major limitations identified in Sec. 1 forExploratory
BI. In terms ofcomputational efficiency, we have
demonstrated in Sec. 6 thatExBIefficiently handles
complex queries by leveraging sampling techniques.

In this paper, we address critical limitations of tra-
ditional BI systems—high dependency on analyst ex-
pertise, static schemas, computational costs, and lack
of reusability—by introducingExploratory BI, a novel
paradigm enabling iterative, incremental analysis. We
presentExBI, a system built on a Hypergraph Data
Model with operators (Source,Join,View, and OLAP
operators) that support dynamic schema evolution and
intermediate result reuse. To achieve real-time respon-
siveness, we implement sampling-based optimizations
with provable theoretical guarantees for the estimation.
Experimental results on the LDBC datasets demon-
strate thatExBIachieves significant speedups: on av-
erage 16.21×compared to Neo4j and 46.67×compared
to MySQL, while maintaining high accuracy with an
average error rate of only 0.27% forCOUNT.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evolution of Business Intelligence (BI) analysis towards Exploratory BI, an iterative and multi-round exploration paradigm. It highlights the limitations of traditional BI systems, including heavy reliance on expert knowledge, rigid schemas, lack of reusability, and high computational costs. The document introduces a novel system called ExBI, which utilizes a hypergraph data model with operators to enable dynamic schema evolution and materialized view reuse. ExBI addresses computational bottlenecks using sampling-based algorithms with provable estimation guarantees, maintaining analytical accuracy. The system is demonstrated to achieve significant speedups over existing systems, with an average error rate of 0.27% for COUNT queries, enabling efficient and accurate large-scale exploratory BI workflows.

nodes.title: Motivations
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of traditional Business Intelligence (BI) tools in supporting exploratory scenarios, particularly in handling complex data relationships and iterative analysis. It highlights four critical limitations of existing BI tools: heavy reliance on analyst expertise, rigid schema, lack of reusability, and computational cost. The document proposes a novel BI system, ExBI, which addresses these challenges through a hypergraph data model, dynamic schema evolution, and sampling-based execution engine. ExBI enables incremental exploration, reduces dependency on analyst expertise, and provides theoretical guarantees for unbiased estimation of aggregate functions. The system is evaluated through extensive experiments on LDBC datasets, demonstrating significant performance improvements and excellent estimation accuracy compared to existing systems.

nodes.title: Our Approach
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a novel business intelligence system called ExBI, designed to support exploratory business intelligence scenarios. The system decouples the logical exploration model from the physical storage layer, addressing challenges such as expressiveness, flexibility, and computational bottlenecks. ExBI introduces a hypergraph data model and operators to support dynamic schema evolution, reusable intermediate views, and operator composition for incremental exploration. The system also tackles performance bottlenecks through a specialized, sampling-based execution engine, providing theoretical guarantees for unbiased estimation of aggregate functions. The document highlights the contributions of the paper, including the introduction of the exploratory BI paradigm, the hypergraph data model and operators, the system implementation with theoretical guarantees, and experimental evaluation showing significant performance improvements over existing systems.
